title: Oaths and Vows: WCF Chapter 22
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-05-23 - Oaths and Vows - WCF 22 - Pastor TIto Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why This Matters
subpoints: WCF Chapter 22 addresses oaths (paragraphs 1-4) and vows (paragraphs 5-7)
An oath = a promise made to people, calling God as witness to its truth
A vow = a promise made directly to God
At the time of the Westminster Assembly there was great controversy over oaths; the subject remains important because of our culture's low view of truth and forgotten importance of promises

outline.title: 1. What an Oath Is and Why It Is an Act of Worship
subpoints: An oath is calling God as witness to the truthfulness of what we are saying or promising (Deut. 10:20)
Taking an oath calls on God's omniscience (He knows if we lie) and omnipresence (He is present to witness anywhere)
A.A. Hodge: an oath recognizes God's omniscience, omnipresence, absolute justice, and sovereignty
Because an oath invokes God's name and presence, it is an act of worship
Closely related to the third commandment: we must not take the Lord's name in vain

outline.title: 2. Only God's Name May Be Invoked in an Oath
subpoints: Deuteronomy 6:13: 'By His name you shall swear' — this is the verse Jesus quoted to resist Satan's temptation to worship
To swear by anything other than God's name is to attribute God's attributes to another — a form of worship of a false god
This includes swearing by saints (as in Roman Catholicism)
Jesus in Matthew 5:33-37 and James 5:12: let your yes be yes and your no be no — be a truth-telling person who doesn't need oaths to be believed

outline.title: 3. The Duty and Propriety of Taking Lawful Oaths
subpoints: The Bible does not forbid oaths — God Himself has taken oaths (Isaiah 45:23; Hebrews 6)
Paul invokes God as witness (2 Cor. 1:23; Rom. 1:9); Paul adjures Timothy to preach faithfully
Jesus, when adjured by the high priest, spoke — recognizing the lawful authority of the court
WCF: oaths are proper when imposed by lawful authority and when the matter is of sufficient weight

outline.title: 4. An Oath Must Be Taken Without Equivocation
subpoints: We must not willfully use words we know the other party understands differently than we do
Mental reservation — making a promise while privately holding a different definition — is manipulation
Psalm 24:4: 'He who does not swear deceitfully' is the righteous man
If in doubt that your meaning is understood, take time to define terms before making the promise

outline.title: 5. When an Oath Is Binding
subpoints: A lawful oath is binding even to our own hurt — Psalm 15:4
An oath is binding regardless of to whom it was made — even to heretics and infidels
A sinful oath is not binding: we cannot call God as witness to something sinful; breaking such an oath to conform to God's law is right
Rash oaths (lawful in themselves but foolish) are still binding, though God calls us to repentance for the rashness

outline.title: 6. Vows: Promises Made Directly to God
subpoints: Vows are scriptural (Ps. 116:12-14; Eccl. 5:4-5) — Christ Himself made vows
You cannot vow to do what is beyond your power or what is sinful
You cannot vow on behalf of others
Roman Catholic perpetual vows of celibacy, poverty, and obedience are rejected as contrary to Scripture

outline.title: 7. Application: Church Membership Vows
subpoints: The four membership vows of the congregation are oaths — solemn promises made with God as witness
Vow 1: 'Do you trust in Christ alone for your salvation?' — calling God to witness that Christ is your only hope
Vow 2: 'Do you promise to support this congregation with your talents, time, and tithes?' — commitment to attend all services, give regularly and without designation, and serve
Vow 3: 'Do you pledge submission to the government of this church as long as it follows Scripture?' — Hebrews 13:17
Vow 4: 'Do you pledge to study the peace of the church in areas of disagreement?' — no division; openness to instruction

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro teaches through Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 22 on oaths and vows. He explains the distinction between an oath (promise to people with God as witness) and a vow (promise made directly to God), demonstrates that taking an oath is an act of worship invoking God's omniscience and omnipresence, and establishes that only God's name may be lawfully invoked. He covers the duty of taking lawful oaths, the requirement of plain speech without equivocation, and the binding nature of oaths — even to one's own hurt. He applies the teaching directly to the four membership vows of the church.
transcript:
## Introduction

Today we take up a standalone lesson on Chapter 22 of the Westminster Confession of Faith, which addresses the subject of oaths and vows. Around the time of the writing of the Confession, there was considerable controversy over this issue, which is why the Westminster divines included it among the thirty-plus doctrines they address. Though those controversies have largely subsided, the subject remains important — both because of how our culture thinks about truth, and because we ourselves have forgotten the seriousness of the promises we make.

Chapter 22 divides into two sections: paragraphs 1-4 address oaths, and paragraphs 5-7 address vows.

The key distinction right at the outset: **an oath** is a promise made to another person, calling God as witness to its truth. **A vow** is a promise made directly to God.

## 1. What an Oath Is and Why It Is an Act of Worship

Westminster paragraph 1: *A lawful oath is a part of religious worship, wherein, upon just occasion, the person swearing solemnly calleth God to witness what he asserteth or promiseth, and to judge him according to the truth or falsehood of what he sweareth.*

Deuteronomy 10:20: *You shall fear the Lord your God; you shall serve Him, and to Him you shall hold fast, and by His name you shall swear.*

An oath calls on God's **omniscience** — He knows whether we are telling the truth. It calls on His **omnipresence** — He is present everywhere to witness any oath at any time, among any number of people simultaneously. A.A. Hodge noted that an oath recognizes the omnipresence, omniscience, absolute justice, and sovereignty of God.

Because an oath invokes God's name and presence, it is an act of worship. This connects directly to the third commandment: *You shall not take the name of the Lord your God in vain.* Every time we say "I swear," even without using God's name explicitly, we are calling on His presence and attributes.

## 2. Only God's Name May Be Invoked

Westminster paragraph 2: *The name of God only is that by which men ought to swear, and therein it is to be used with all holy fear and reverence.*

Deuteronomy 6:13: *The Lord your God you shall fear; Him you shall serve, and by His name you shall swear.*

This is the verse Jesus quoted to resist Satan's temptation to worship (*'You shall worship the Lord your God, and Him only shall you serve'*). To swear by anything other than God's name — by heaven, by Jerusalem, by the saints — is to attribute God's attributes to another. It is, in a meaningful sense, idolatry.

Jesus in Matthew 5:33-37 and James in 5:12 address this from a different angle: let your yes be yes and your no be no. Be the kind of person whose word is trustworthy without the need for oaths to be believed. The problem Jesus addressed was the Pharisees' casuistry — inventing ways to make promises that sounded binding but were not.

## 3. The Duty and Propriety of Oaths

The Bible does not forbid the taking of oaths. God Himself has taken oaths:

> "By Myself I have sworn... that to Me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall take an oath." (Isaiah 45:23)

In Hebrews 6, the author explains that God confirmed His covenant with Abraham by swearing by Himself — because He could swear by nothing greater. Paul invokes God as witness multiple times (2 Cor. 1:23; Rom. 1:9). Paul adjured Timothy to preach faithfully. Jesus, when adjured by the high priest at His trial, ceased His silence and answered — recognizing the lawful authority of a court to impose an oath.

So oaths are not inherently sinful. They are appropriate — sometimes even obligatory — when imposed by lawful authority and when the matter is of sufficient weight.

## 4. An Oath Must Be Without Equivocation

Westminster paragraph 4: *An oath is to be taken in the plain and common sense of the words, without equivocation or mental reservation.*

We must not willfully use words that we know will be understood differently by the other party than what we intend. This is manipulation. Psalm 24:4 describes the righteous man as one who "does not swear deceitfully." If there is any doubt that your meaning is understood, take the time to define your terms before making the promise.

## 5. When an Oath Is Binding

Westminster paragraph 3-4: a lawful oath, though it hurt us, binds us to performance. Psalm 15:4: *He who swears to his own hurt and does not change.*

Furthermore, it does not matter who we made the promise to. The obligation rests on the one who took the oath, not on the character of the recipient. Even oaths made to heretics and infidels — a specific issue in the Reformation era, when Rome taught that promises to Protestants need not be kept — are binding.

On the other hand, a sinful oath is not binding. We cannot call God as witness to something that is itself sinful. Breaking such an oath to conform to God's law is right — indeed, required.

## 6. Vows: Promises Made to God

Vows are scripturally warranted. Psalm 116:12-14; Ecclesiastes 5:4-5; Christ Himself made vows. But vows carry their own constraints:

- You cannot vow to do what is beyond your power.
- You cannot vow on behalf of others.
- You cannot vow to do what is sinful.

Westminster rejects Roman Catholic perpetual vows of celibacy, poverty, and religious obedience as contrary to Scripture — not because the commitments are inherently wrong, but because they are presented as paths to higher holiness, which Scripture does not teach.

## Application: The Four Church Membership Vows

When you become a member of this congregation, you take four vows — solemn promises made with God as witness.

**Vow 1**: *Do you trust in Christ alone for your salvation?* You are calling God to witness that Christ and His righteousness — not your own works — is your only hope.

**Vow 2**: *Do you promise to support this congregation with your talents, time, and tithes?* You are committing to attend all services (not merely when convenient), to give regularly without designation, and to serve in the life of the church.

**Vow 3**: *Do you pledge submission to the government of this church as long as it follows the dictates of Scripture?* Hebrews 13:17: *Obey those who rule over you, and be submissive* — the same word used for children obeying parents. This is not optional for the American who values autonomy.

**Vow 4**: *Do you pledge to study the peace of the church in areas where you disagree with the doctrinal standards?* You are promising not to create divisions, to study the confession carefully, and to remain open to instruction — knowing that these standards have been tested by centuries of wiser men than any of us.

These are not casual commitments. They are oaths. God heard them when you made them, and He holds you to them.
